title: THE HEIR — Act 1 part 2, SINGLE-VOICE performance (minutes ~10-15: the chained door, the checkpoint, the iron offered)
notes: Continues act1-opening-singlevoice. One voice (George) performs all parts; registers per character. Frames are linear and used once each (user rule: never reuse a frame; hold beats flips; pro model only).

NADIR.direction: neutral register; tonight: urgent, arguing for his life with case law

CHIEF JUSTICE.direction: thin, frightened old voice behind a chain

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: [measured, dry] The Chief Justice's house. A good house on a quiet street, every window dark on purpose. Nadir on the step, an overcoat over his funeral suit, a folder under his arm — an actual folder, tabs and flags, assembled in two hours. Bayar waits at the car, letting the boy try.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: The door opens the width of its chain. One old eye.

segments.speaker: NADIR
segments.text: [urgent, reasonable, leaning in] Article eleven. I read the whole constitution on the plane — your constitution, you drafted half of it. The presidency is vacant, the army has no standing to fill it, and you have the lawful power to swear in an interim civil council tonight. Three names. I've written them down. None of them is mine — that's the point, none of them is mine and none of them is his. You convene the court, the succession is legal, and no one has to own the country by morning. [quieter, pressing] It's all here. It's clean. It would hold up anywhere on earth.

segments.speaker: CHIEF JUSTICE
segments.text: [in a thin, frightened old voice] It's a beautiful argument, Mr. Demir. I can hear your father's opposite in every line of it. [the voice wavers] I have grandchildren. Four of them. Three streets from here.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: [measured] The door closes. The bolt goes over, soft, apologetic. Nadir stands on the step holding the cleanest argument of his life.

segments.speaker: BAYAR
segments.text: [old dry register, from a distance, no triumph in it] That was the last law in the country, Mr. President. You just watched it lock its door.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: [measured] A boulevard checkpoint. The car, stopped. Floodlights. Soldiers in field dress — Rustam's — walking the line of waiting vehicles with torches. One of them is checking faces against a phone. In the back seat, Nadir and Bayar. Bayar slides a passport across — not Nadir's.

segments.speaker: BAYAR
segments.text: [old dry register, very quietly] Look bored. Bored is invisible.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: The torch fills the window. A sergeant studies the passport, then the two men — a long, careless, deciding look. Somewhere ahead a car is being emptied, a man spread against its roof, his suitcase open on the asphalt, shirts in the wind. [pause] The sergeant waves them through.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Nadir watches the spread-eagled man slide past his window — a businessman, somebody's uncle, nobody — and Nadir's breath has gone shallow, and he doesn't notice his own hand has closed around the door handle, white.

segments.speaker: BAYAR
segments.text: [old dry register, unhurried] Now you know what it is, when it isn't an argument. That's his country, one checkpoint at a time, by Thursday. Whoever that man is, he is now a person things can be done to. [pause] There is one list in Karash with your name on it tonight, Mr. President. The question is whether it's his or mine.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: [measured] Bayar's office, the Office itself. The offer still sitting on him — and the checkpoint sitting on him harder. Bayar pours a drink Nadir doesn't touch. General Turan is here too — older, scarred, the army's honest man, and the fact that he walked into Bayar's office instead of Rustam's is its own kind of vote.

segments.speaker: NADIR
segments.text: [worn, grasping] He said he'd let me leave. Sign it over, and I leave.

segments.speaker: TURAN
segments.text: [blunt soldier's growl] He said you'd stay on as his guest. Did you catch the part where you leave? I served beside that man thirty years. I never once saw him let a guest walk back out a door.

segments.speaker: NADIR
segments.text: [thin] So I sign nothing, and I sit here, and I wait for him to —

segments.speaker: BAYAR
segments.text: [old dry register, cutting in, deliberate] You don't wait. There is no version of this night where waiting is one of the moves. By sunrise the country has Rustam or it has you, and the difference, to a hundred thousand people who will never know your name, is whether the next ten years are a boot or an open hand. [pause] I'm not asking you to want the chair. I gave up expecting men to want it a long time ago. I'm telling you the only people who walk out of this alive are the ones who pick it up before it's handed to them.

segments.speaker: NADIR
segments.text: [desperate, almost laughing] And how. I'm a lawyer. I don't have an army. I have a coat and a closed airport.

segments.speaker: BAYAR
segments.text: [old dry register, the blade arriving slowly] You have the one thing Rustam will never have, which is the right to the chair, and you have me, and I know where his men sleep and which of them he's quietly stopped paying. Your father wouldn't have asked how. Your father, by now, would have a list. [pause] The question isn't whether you can. It's whether you can do it the way it has to be done and still call yourself the man who landed tonight. [quieter] I already know the answer. So do you. You just haven't said it where you could hear it.
